title: End of module: Databases and SQL

questions.text: In the relational model, which formal term refers to a **column** of a table?
questions.explanation: A column is also called an **attribute** (or field). A row is a **record** or **tuple**; a **foreign key** is a particular kind of column, not the general term for any column.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly defines a table's **primary key**?
choices: Any column that contains only numbers
A column (or set of columns) whose value is unique and never null, used to identify each row
The first column listed in the table
A column that copies values from another table
questions.explanation: A primary key must be unique across all rows and never null, so every row can be identified unambiguously. Option 4 describes a **foreign key**, not a primary key.

questions.text: A **foreign key** in table A is best described as:
choices: A column in table A that is never allowed to repeat
A column in table A that stores a primary key value from table B, creating a relationship between A and B
Any column shared between two tables with the same name
The primary key of table A itself
questions.explanation: A foreign key stores another table's primary key value, linking the two tables — e.g. `Student.TutorID` referencing `Tutor.TutorID`.

questions.text: A school stores all its enrolment data in a single flat table (one row per student-and-course combination, with the student's and tutor's details repeated on every row). Which of the following are genuine problems this design causes? Select all that apply.
choices: Update anomaly — changing a student's e-mail means updating every row that mentions them
The table takes up strictly less storage than splitting it into several normalised tables
Insertion anomaly — a new course cannot be recorded until a student has enrolled on it
Deletion anomaly — removing a student's last enrolment on a course can delete facts about the tutor that have nothing to do with that student
questions.explanation: Repeating the same tutor/student facts on many rows causes update, insertion and deletion anomalies (options 1, 3 and 4). It does not save storage — the opposite is true, since normalisation removes the very duplication that wastes space.

questions.text: A table has a **composite** primary key (OrderID, ProductID). A non-key column, ProductName, depends only on ProductID — not on the whole key. Which normal form does this violate?
choices: It doesn't violate any normal form
questions.explanation: A non-key column depending on only part of a composite key is a **partial dependency**, which violates 2NF. Fix it by moving ProductName into its own table keyed on ProductID.

questions.text: A `Course` table is keyed on CourseID and is already in 2NF. It also stores TutorID, TutorName and TutorRoom, where TutorName and TutorRoom depend on TutorID rather than directly on CourseID. Which normal form does this violate?
choices: It doesn't violate any normal form
questions.explanation: CourseID → TutorID → {TutorName, TutorRoom} is a **transitive dependency** (a non-key column depending on another non-key column), which violates 3NF. Fix it by moving TutorName and TutorRoom into their own Tutor table.

questions.text: Which SQL statement changes the values in a row that already exists, without adding or removing any rows?
questions.explanation: `UPDATE … SET … WHERE …` changes existing values in matched rows. `INSERT INTO` adds a new row; `DELETE` removes rows; `SELECT` only reads data.

questions.text: Which SQL statement adds a brand-new row to a table?
questions.explanation: `INSERT INTO Table (columns) VALUES (values);` adds a new row. `ORDER BY` is a clause used with `SELECT`, not a statement of its own.

questions.text: Complete the missing SQL keyword: `SELECT StudentName ____ Student WHERE Age = 17;`
questions.explanation: Every `SELECT` needs a `FROM` clause naming the table to read from: `SELECT StudentName FROM Student WHERE Age = 17;`.

questions.text: Which two-word SQL clause would you add to `SELECT StudentName, Age FROM Student` to sort the results by Age?
questions.explanation: `ORDER BY Age` sorts the result rows by the Age column (ascending by default).

questions.text: Using this Tutor table — T1: Dr Patel, Room R12; T2: Mr Owusu, Room R7; T3: Ms Grant, Room R15 — and this Student table — S1 Alice Chen, Age 17, TutorID T1; S2 Ben Okafor, Age 16, TutorID T1; S3 Chloe Ahmed, Age 17, TutorID T2; S4 Dan Wills, Age 16, TutorID T3; S5 Eva Novak, Age 17, TutorID T2 — how many rows does `SELECT * FROM Student WHERE Age = 17;` return?
questions.explanation: Age = 17 for S1 (Alice Chen), S3 (Chloe Ahmed) and S5 (Eva Novak) — 3 rows. S2 and S4 are both 16, so they are excluded.

questions.text: Using the same Tutor and Student tables as the previous question, how many rows does this query return? `SELECT Student.StudentName, Tutor.TutorName FROM Student INNER JOIN Tutor ON Student.TutorID = Tutor.TutorID;`
questions.explanation: Every one of the 5 students has exactly one matching tutor (S1, S2 → T1; S3, S5 → T2; S4 → T3), so the INNER JOIN produces exactly one output row per student: 5 rows in total.
